content.assessment: Compensatory investment in comprehensive schools requires no change to the statutory track framework and avoids the maintainer-autonomy conflicts inherent to structural or admissions reform, since it operates through resource allocation to public comprehensive schools rather than through governance changes affecting church, private, or track-holding institutions. This makes it the least legally contentious proposal, though its accountability mechanism for actually reducing selective-track demand remains unproven.
opportunities: Investment authority already resides with existing public-school governance structures, requiring no new legal instruments
strengths: Requires no amendment to Act CXC and does not implicate maintainer autonomy of church or private gimnáziums, since it targets comprehensive-school resourcing directly

Empirical association between high-poverty school attendance and reduced reading/attainment outcomes provides a defensible evidentiary basis for targeted public investment authority [F-live-legal_and_governance-04]
threats: Absent any linkage to admissions or structural reform, this proposal risks perpetual deferral of the governance questions raised by the Minister's own stated concerns about selection
weaknesses: No governance mechanism links quality investment to reduced demand for selective tracks, leaving the causal chain underspecified for accountability purposes
